quote_of_day.text: You know you live in one of the worlds biggest shithole where you are successfully taught to believe the worth of your feeble existence entirely hinges on a arbitrary written test and how well you perform on it determines your value and social status in a low IQ low trust soyciety
quote_of_day.name: Anon
quote_of_day.topic: existential despair in a low-trust society

articles.title: Weather on the board today
articles.body:
A low-pressure system of paternal anxiety moved into the board this morning, with one anon expressing concern over daughters dancing on Instagram. The post drew a squall of responses. accumulating.

A front of skepticism about India's rape statistics blew in later, as an anon claimed the country is not the 'rape capital' it is made out to be.

The discussion was marked by scattered showers of data and counter-claims. A related cold snap of criticism towards those making rape-related claims also moved in.

Meanwhile, a high-pressure ridge of tech frustration developed as anons reacted to the Telegram ban. A drizzle of complaints and jokes about the ban's impact on NEET exam preparation persisted throughout the day.

A brief fog of controversy also rolled in after an anon sparked outrage with an 'Allah Hu Akbar' post.

articles.title: Fathers and Daughters on Instagram
articles.body:
Anon expressed concern about fathers allowing their daughters to post half-naked dance videos on Instagram. He wondered if they had any masculinity left, suggesting that thousands of men were jerking off to these videos, degrading the daughters.

Anon replied that the fathers' behavior was influenced by their wives, who had been exposed to Western culture but not enough to live it. These women, according to Anon, now try to live vicariously through their daughters.

Another Anon added that historically, temple dancing in India was associated with certain hereditary classes, mostly women of the Isai Vellalar caste. Himachali gunda suggested that some men are naturally passive and avoid conflict, prioritizing being liked over societal concerns.

This passivity, he implied, leads them to allow their daughters to pursue careers in dance. Anon countered that many fathers are aware of their daughters' multiple sex partners but do nothing.

Anon also discussed the caste dynamics of professional dancers on Instagram, claiming that less than 10 Brahmins are among the top 100 dance influencers. Most, according to him, are from lower castes.

Another Anon noted that even in ancient Rome, professional dancers and actors were looked down upon, and today's Instagram dance influencers continue to be sexualized.

The conversation also touched on the idea that urban, affluent, and educated people tend to be more liberal, which Himachali gunda illustrated by referencing the Jewish community.

Anon argued that this liberalism doesn't change the fact that many dance influencers are from lower castes. Anon shared an anecdote about an army man's daughter who became a sex worker in the NCR, highlighting the consequences of such choices.

The discussion remained focused on the perceived failures of masculinity and the societal implications of these choices.

articles.title: Rape Capital Claims Spark Debate
articles.body:
Anon kicked off the discussion by claiming that India being labeled the 'rape capital' is a significant psyop.

They argued that India has a low per capita rape rate, with many cases being fake, and that the true rate is even lower than that of Europe or developed countries.

Another anon countered that while India may not be the rape capital, it is 'rapey enough.' This poster also suggested that African countries have higher rates of rape.

In response, an anon cited personal experience, stating that they and their cousin sister ride bikes at 2 am in upper-caste neighborhoods without incident, but noted that catcalling can occur in crowded areas.

The conversation took a darker turn when an anon mentioned that most rapes go unreported due to social stigma and the protection of family honor. This poster proposed documenting all rape incidents to create a comprehensive record.

Others chimed in, with one suggesting that only certain groups, such as 'chamars and mullahs,' are prone to rape.

The thread also saw the introduction of unrelated claims, including an assertion that feminism is a form of terrorism and that many reported rape cases are fake or exaggerated.

Anon participants exchanged views on the portrayal of India and Hinduism, with some accusing others of being misinformed or influenced by 'psyops.'

articles.title: Anons React to Telegram Ban
articles.body:
Anon kicked off the discussion by calling for the unban of 'absolute nincompoops' after the NEET exam, which quickly took a turn towards the recently banned Telegram.

Anon questioned why everyone was upset about Telegram getting banned, comparing it to WhatsApp. Another anon explained that Telegram was unique because it had 'niche content' that couldn't be found elsewhere, including war-related information and study materials.

One anon noted that Telegram was a 'legit free speech app' that allowed Russians to post information about Putin's recruitment of teenage boys, and that it had no algorithm like other social media platforms.

However, another anon countered that Telegram wasn't as 'free' as it seemed, citing its ban on piracy and free-think bots and channels.

The discussion also touched on the potential replacement for Telegram, with an anon expressing doubt that a suitable alternative would be found. Another anon joked that Indians could create their own communities and copy content from Telegram.

The reasons behind the ban were also questioned, with an anon suggesting that it was due to censorship ordered by the government.

Others speculated that the government might be trying to control the flow of information or that Telegram was banned due to its association with 'anti-national activity'.

The thread also included some humorous exchanges, with one anon poking fun at those who used Telegram for 'dehati porn' and another missing the 'unsausable videos' on the platform.

articles.title: Anon sparks controversy with 'Allah Hu Akbar' post
articles.body: Anon started the thread with a simple 'Allah Hu Akbar' post. One anon responded with 'you done fucked up mudslime' and an obscene remark about Allah. Another anon asked if the original poster had only read the heading, calling it 'TOPKEK' and 'memes writing themselves'. Replies included claims about a movie called Kantara, and insults about Islam and Muslims.

articles.title: Anon Bemoans Dosa Service
articles.body:
Anon posted about buying four masala dosas from a shop, wondering if the vendor was staring at their stomach.

The vendor allegedly checked on their chutney and sambar 10 and 5 times, respectively.

One respondent joked that the cast of the dosas might have been the issue, estimating the cost at 360 rupees.

Another Anon shared that they lost 9 kgs of weight since visiting the city, missing home-cooked food.

A separate Anon boasted of eating 15 idlis in one meal, prompting another to claim they could only eat 6 of the 250-gram idlis from their favorite shop.
